title: IV Cannulation
oneLiner: Insertion of a plastic catheter into a peripheral vein for IV fluids, medications, or blood sampling; performed daily on most inpatients.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Air embolism:** 0.5–1 mL/kg entrained air can cause right heart outflow obstruction → sudden hypotension, cyanosis, mill-wheel murmur. **Single finding:** sudden drop in ETCO₂ and hypotension during insertion/removal with an open catheter hub.
- **Arterial cannulation (unrecognised):** high-pressure flow with pulsatile flashback, rapid flushing causes forearm ischaemia, compartment syndrome, distal necrosis. **Single finding:** bright red pulsatile flashback + inability to flush with gentle pressure + arterial waveform if transduced.
sections.Presentation:
- **Indications for insertion:** IV fluids (maintenance/resuscitation), IV medications, blood product transfusion, contrast for imaging, IV access for emergencies.
- **Pre-procedure assessment:** inspect visible veins (antecubital fossa, dorsum of hand, forearm), palpate for non-thrombosed, elastic veins. Look for contraindications (AV fistula, lymphoedema, mastectomy side, cellulitis over site, burns).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Easy access** (visible, bouncy vein) → successful cannulation in <2 attempts.
- **Difficult access** (obese, dehydrated, IVDU, chemotherapy) → requires ultrasound-guided or central line.
- **Splitter:** vein feels hard and cord-like → thrombophlebitis — do NOT cannulate; use another site.
sections.Investigations:
(Not applicable in the diagnostic sense; instead, pre-insertion checks)

- **Bedside:** gauge selection — 18G for blood/rapid fluids, 20G for routine fluids/drugs, 22G for fragile veins. Check tourniquet pressure, hand hygiene, PPE (gloves, apron). Confirm patient ID with allergy status (latex, chlorhexidine, lidocaine).
- **Bloods:** none required before routine cannulation unless checking coagulation if high bleeding risk (warfarin, INR>3, severe thrombocytopenia <20). Consider bleeding risk – but generally safe with gentle pressure.
- **Imaging:** ultrasound (USS) for difficult access — identify target vein depth and patency.
- **Never-miss test:** **Check skin over intended site** — if erythema, warmth, pus → do NOT insert; that site is infected. Also check mastectomy/AV fistula side — document existing vascular access.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  1. Gather equipment: sterile cannula, tourniquet, chlorhexidine 2% in 70% alcohol wipe, saline flush, Tegaderm/sterile dressing, tape.
  2. Apply tourniquet ~5-10cm above site. Select vein.
  3. Clean skin with chlorhexidine — allow to dry 30 seconds.
  4. Stabilise vein with non-dominant hand; puncture skin at 10-30°, bevel up.
  5. Observe flashback; lower angle and advance 2–3mm into vein. Advance catheter over needle, remove needle directly into sharps.
  6. Release tourniquet. Attach saline-filled extension set/ syringe; flush gently to confirm patency.
  7. Secure with transparent dressing + tape. Document date, size, site.
  8. Consider local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% 0.1-0.2mL intradermal) for anxious patients — wait 1-2 minutes.

- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Observe site daily for phlebitis (redness, pain, swelling, palpable cord). Replace if clinical signs or >72-96 hours (local policy may differ). For continuous infusions, change giving set every 24h.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** For PICC or midline insertion (long-term >7d), refer to IV access team. Not for routine peripheral cannula.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** as prescribed (e.g., Hartmann’s 1L/8h for maintenance if NBM).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if not contraindicated) – independent of cannula.
  - **Cannula care:** flush with 5mL 0.9% saline after each use. Lock with heparinised saline if policy (e.g., 10 units/mL [check local policy]).
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 per trust protocol; inspect IV site at least Q8h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Flushing agent:** 0.9% sodium chloride — 5-10mL pre- and post- drug administration.
- **Local anaesthetic (optional, for difficult sticks):** lidocaine 1% (max dose 3 mg/kg with adrenaline, 4.5 mg/kg without; typical 0.1-0.2mL intradermal). [Check local policy for specific brand/pre-filled syringe availability].
- **Heparin lock (for intermittent use):** 10 units/mL in 5mL [check local policy] — not needed in all trusts.
- **Key contraindication / interaction:** lidocaine – caution in severe hepatic impairment, heart block, epilepsy. Heparin – check for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) history.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** lidocaine – dose reduction in severe hepatic impairment (reduce max dose by 50%). Heparin – no renal adjustment, but monitor for bleeding if eGFR<30.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Managing heparin lock** – accidentally injecting heparin 1000 units/mL (ICU concentration) via peripheral cannula can cause massive overdose and bleeding. Always check label: *“Heparin 10 units/mL” vs “1000 units/mL”*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** haematoma (apply pressure 2-3 min), arterial puncture (immediate removal + firm pressure 5 min + call senior if not controlled), air embolism (place patient left lateral head-down, O₂, call crash team), nerve injury (sharp shooting pain — remove immediately, document), extravasation of irritant drug (stop, aspirate, warm compress, call senior).
- **Chronic:** phlebitis (superficial thrombophlebitis — warm compress, consider removal), infection (cellulitis, catheter-related bloodstream infection — remove cannula, send tip for culture, start antibiotics per local policy), venous scarring (multiple attempts → thrombosed veins).
sections.Prognosis:
- First-attempt success ~60-80% for new starters, >90% for experienced clinicians. Most failed attempts due to poor vein selection or malposition.
- If >2 attempts, success rate drops; consider using ultrasound or calling IV access team.
- Cannula dwell time limited to 72-96h typically; later removal reduces infection risk.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **“Always anchor the vein”** — stretch skin distally with your non-dominant thumb to prevent rolling during insertion.
- **Use a blood pressure cuff instead of tourniquet** in elderly fragile veins — inflate to 30-40 mmHg for consistent venous distention without pressure injury.
- **If flashback but no further flow** — you may have gone through the back wall; withdraw needle slightly, re-advance catheter. If still fails, remove and apply pressure.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not** re-site a cannula into the same vein just distal to a previous stick — high risk of haematoma and occlusion.
- **Do not** flush against resistance — you may break valve or cause extravasation. Check patency with gentle aspiration first.
- **Do not** leave the introducer needle uncapped or unattended — immediate sharps disposal prevents needlestick injury.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** cannula site becomes red, hot, painful, or oozes pus; if you develop fever or chills; if you experience sudden shortness of breath or chest pain (possible air embolism or clot); if arm becomes swollen, pale, or cool (arterial compromise).
- **Follow-up:** Community IV therapy team if ongoing IV access required; otherwise remove cannula at discharge.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior** (SpR/IV access team) if: two failed attempts by experienced clinician; patient has known difficult IV access or no palpable veins; need for central line (PICC/CVC) because no peripheral access.
- **Call crash / ICU** if: suspected air embolism with hypotension and hypoxia; suspected anaphylaxis to lidocaine or chlorhexidine (urticaria, stridor, shock); arterial bleed from stuck artery not controlled by pressure.

rawMarkdown:
## IV Cannulation

**One-liner:** Insertion of a plastic catheter into a peripheral vein for IV fluids, medications, or blood sampling; performed daily on most inpatients.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Air embolism:** 0.5–1 mL/kg entrained air can cause right heart outflow obstruction → sudden hypotension, cyanosis, mill-wheel murmur. **Single finding:** sudden drop in ETCO₂ and hypotension during insertion/removal with an open catheter hub.
- **Arterial cannulation (unrecognised):** high-pressure flow with pulsatile flashback, rapid flushing causes forearm ischaemia, compartment syndrome, distal necrosis. **Single finding:** bright red pulsatile flashback + inability to flush with gentle pressure + arterial waveform if transduced.

### Presentation

- **Indications for insertion:** IV fluids (maintenance/resuscitation), IV medications, blood product transfusion, contrast for imaging, IV access for emergencies.
- **Pre-procedure assessment:** inspect visible veins (antecubital fossa, dorsum of hand, forearm), palpate for non-thrombosed, elastic veins. Look for contraindications (AV fistula, lymphoedema, mastectomy side, cellulitis over site, burns).

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Easy access** (visible, bouncy vein) → successful cannulation in <2 attempts.
- **Difficult access** (obese, dehydrated, IVDU, chemotherapy) → requires ultrasound-guided or central line.
- **Splitter:** vein feels hard and cord-like → thrombophlebitis — do NOT cannulate; use another site.

### Investigations

(Not applicable in the diagnostic sense; instead, pre-insertion checks)

- **Bedside:** gauge selection — 18G for blood/rapid fluids, 20G for routine fluids/drugs, 22G for fragile veins. Check tourniquet pressure, hand hygiene, PPE (gloves, apron). Confirm patient ID with allergy status (latex, chlorhexidine, lidocaine).
- **Bloods:** none required before routine cannulation unless checking coagulation if high bleeding risk (warfarin, INR>3, severe thrombocytopenia <20). Consider bleeding risk – but generally safe with gentle pressure.
- **Imaging:** ultrasound (USS) for difficult access — identify target vein depth and patency.
- **Never-miss test:** **Check skin over intended site** — if erythema, warmth, pus → do NOT insert; that site is infected. Also check mastectomy/AV fistula side — document existing vascular access.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  1. Gather equipment: sterile cannula, tourniquet, chlorhexidine 2% in 70% alcohol wipe, saline flush, Tegaderm/sterile dressing, tape.
  2. Apply tourniquet ~5-10cm above site. Select vein.
  3. Clean skin with chlorhexidine — allow to dry 30 seconds.
  4. Stabilise vein with non-dominant hand; puncture skin at 10-30°, bevel up.
  5. Observe flashback; lower angle and advance 2–3mm into vein. Advance catheter over needle, remove needle directly into sharps.
  6. Release tourniquet. Attach saline-filled extension set/ syringe; flush gently to confirm patency.
  7. Secure with transparent dressing + tape. Document date, size, site.
  8. Consider local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% 0.1-0.2mL intradermal) for anxious patients — wait 1-2 minutes.

- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Observe site daily for phlebitis (redness, pain, swelling, palpable cord). Replace if clinical signs or >72-96 hours (local policy may differ). For continuous infusions, change giving set every 24h.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** For PICC or midline insertion (long-term >7d), refer to IV access team. Not for routine peripheral cannula.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** as prescribed (e.g., Hartmann’s 1L/8h for maintenance if NBM).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if not contraindicated) – independent of cannula.
  - **Cannula care:** flush with 5mL 0.9% saline after each use. Lock with heparinised saline if policy (e.g., 10 units/mL [check local policy]).
  - **Observation:** NEWS2 per trust protocol; inspect IV site at least Q8h.

### Drugs & Doses

- **Flushing agent:** 0.9% sodium chloride — 5-10mL pre- and post- drug administration.
- **Local anaesthetic (optional, for difficult sticks):** lidocaine 1% (max dose 3 mg/kg with adrenaline, 4.5 mg/kg without; typical 0.1-0.2mL intradermal). [Check local policy for specific brand/pre-filled syringe availability].
- **Heparin lock (for intermittent use):** 10 units/mL in 5mL [check local policy] — not needed in all trusts.
- **Key contraindication / interaction:** lidocaine – caution in severe hepatic impairment, heart block, epilepsy. Heparin – check for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) history.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** lidocaine – dose reduction in severe hepatic impairment (reduce max dose by 50%). Heparin – no renal adjustment, but monitor for bleeding if eGFR<30.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Managing heparin lock** – accidentally injecting heparin 1000 units/mL (ICU concentration) via peripheral cannula can cause massive overdose and bleeding. Always check label: *“Heparin 10 units/mL” vs “1000 units/mL”*

### Complications

- **Acute:** haematoma (apply pressure 2-3 min), arterial puncture (immediate removal + firm pressure 5 min + call senior if not controlled), air embolism (place patient left lateral head-down, O₂, call crash team), nerve injury (sharp shooting pain — remove immediately, document), extravasation of irritant drug (stop, aspirate, warm compress, call senior).
- **Chronic:** phlebitis (superficial thrombophlebitis — warm compress, consider removal), infection (cellulitis, catheter-related bloodstream infection — remove cannula, send tip for culture, start antibiotics per local policy), venous scarring (multiple attempts → thrombosed veins).

### Prognosis

- First-attempt success ~60-80% for new starters, >90% for experienced clinicians. Most failed attempts due to poor vein selection or malposition.
- If >2 attempts, success rate drops; consider using ultrasound or calling IV access team.
- Cannula dwell time limited to 72-96h typically; later removal reduces infection risk.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **“Always anchor the vein”** — stretch skin distally with your non-dominant thumb to prevent rolling during insertion.
- **Use a blood pressure cuff instead of tourniquet** in elderly fragile veins — inflate to 30-40 mmHg for consistent venous distention without pressure injury.
- **If flashback but no further flow** — you may have gone through the back wall; withdraw needle slightly, re-advance catheter. If still fails, remove and apply pressure.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not** re-site a cannula into the same vein just distal to a previous stick — high risk of haematoma and occlusion.
- **Do not** flush against resistance — you may break valve or cause extravasation. Check patency with gentle aspiration first.
- **Do not** leave the introducer needle uncapped or unattended — immediate sharps disposal prevents needlestick injury.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** cannula site becomes red, hot, painful, or oozes pus; if you develop fever or chills; if you experience sudden shortness of breath or chest pain (possible air embolism or clot); if arm becomes swollen, pale, or cool (arterial compromise).
- **Follow-up:** Community IV therapy team if ongoing IV access required; otherwise remove cannula at discharge.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior** (SpR/IV access team) if: two failed attempts by experienced clinician; patient has known difficult IV access or no palpable veins; need for central line (PICC/CVC) because no peripheral access.
- **Call crash / ICU** if: suspected air embolism with hypotension and hypoxia; suspected anaphylaxis to lidocaine or chlorhexidine (urticaria, stridor, shock); arterial bleed from stuck artery not controlled by pressure.
